title: The trigger of someone raising their hand near your face
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
Your body knew before your mind did

It happens in a flash. Someone is talking to you, maybe even someone you love, and their hand comes up. To gesture, to emphasize a point, to brush hair from their own face. But your body doesn't know that. Your body sees a hand moving toward your face and it screams. Freeze, flinch, duck, brace. Your shoulders hike up to your ears. Your breath catches. Your eyes go wide. And then the shame hits. Why did I just do that? Why can't I be normal? You smile, you laugh it off, you pretend nothing happened. But something did happen. Your nervous system just told you a truth your conscious mind couldn't access.

Here's the thing. That reaction is not a character flaw. It is not a sign that you are broken or weak or overly sensitive. It is a sign that your body learned something, somewhere, that your mind may not even remember. And that learning is stored in your tissues, your muscles, your vagus nerve. It's stored in the parts of your brain that process threat before your thinking brain even gets the memo. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent his career documenting how trauma is held in the body, not the narrative. He showed that animals in the wild shake off threat and return to normal. But humans? We hold it. We freeze it. We store it in the arch of our back, the tension in our jaw, the flinch when a hand moves toward our face.

So let me ask you something. When that hand came up, what did you feel? Not what did you think. What did you feel in your body? A tightening in your chest. A hollow feeling in your stomach. A sudden urge to disappear. That's your nervous system talking. And it's speaking a language older than words.

The neuroscience of a flinch

Stephen Porges developed Polyvagal Theory to explain exactly this kind of response. He described three neural circuits that govern our reaction to safety and danger. The ventral vagal, which is our social engagement system, the one that lets us make eye contact, smile, and feel connected. The sympathetic, which is our fight or flight system, the one that gets us ready to run or fight. And the dorsal vagal, which is our oldest, most primitive circuit, the one that makes us freeze, collapse, or dissociate when threat feels inescapable.

Now think about that hand coming toward your face. Your thinking brain, your prefrontal cortex, knows it's just a gesture. But your nervous system doesn't check with your prefrontal cortex before it acts. It checks with your brainstem, your amygdala, your body memory. And if any part of that system says "danger," you are already in a survival response before your conscious mind can say "wait, that's just my friend."

This is why you can't logic your way out of a trauma response. You can tell yourself a hundred times that you are safe. You can repeat affirmations until you are blue in the face. But if your nervous system hasn't updated its threat assessment, you will keep flinching. Keep freezing. Keep dissociating. Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote a book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), showed through decades of research that trauma is not primarily a memory stored in the mind. It is a physiological response stored in the body. And the body does not forget.

I remember the first time I realized this about myself. I was at a dinner party, talking to a friend, and she raised her hand to make a point about something. I flinched so hard I knocked over my water glass. Everyone looked at me. I laughed, said something stupid about being jumpy, and spent the rest of the night replaying the moment in my head. Why did I do that? I had never been hit in the face. Not that I could remember. But here's what I learned later. My body remembered something my mind didn't. A hand raised in anger, a voice that got loud, a face that came too close. Not a single event, but a pattern. A thousand small moments of threat that my nervous system catalogued without my permission.

Stay with me here. This is not about blaming anyone. This is about understanding that your body is doing its job. It is trying to keep you alive. And the problem is not that it flinches. The problem is that it's still operating on old data. It's like a smoke alarm that goes off every time you cook toast. It's not wrong about smoke. It's just calibrated to a threat that no longer exists.

What your body is actually saying

When someone raises their hand near your face, and you react, your body is saying something specific. It is saying "I have been here before." Not necessarily the exact same situation. But the same feeling. The same powerlessness. The same sense that someone bigger, stronger, or more unpredictable is about to do something you cannot control.

This is where Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation becomes so helpful. She talks about how trauma splits the self into parts. There is the part that goes on with daily life, the part that manages, the part that pretends everything is fine. And then there is the part that still lives in the moment of threat. The part that flinches. The part that freezes. The part that feels small and scared and wants to disappear. That part is not your enemy. That part is a protector. It is trying to keep you safe the only way it knows how.

So when you feel that flinch, try something different. Instead of judging yourself, instead of pushing it away, try to get curious. What does that part need right now? What would it say if it could speak? Sometimes the answer is simple. It needs to know you see it. It needs to know you are not going to ignore it or shame it. It needs to know that you, the adult you, are here now and can handle things.

Wild, right? The idea that you can talk to your own nervous system. That you can reassure the parts of you that are still scared. But this is exactly what the research supports. Dan Siegel talks about integration, the process of linking different parts of the brain and body so they can work together. When you acknowledge the flinch instead of fighting it, you start to integrate. You start to tell your body "I see you. I hear you. We are safe now."

I am not saying this is easy. It is not. It takes practice, patience, and a lot of self-compassion. Some days you will still flinch. Some days the shame will still come. But over time, if you keep showing up with curiosity instead of judgment, something shifts. The flinch becomes less automatic. The shame becomes less intense. You start to notice the hand coming up and you have a tiny moment of choice. A moment where you can breathe, where you can feel your feet on the floor, where you can remind your body that you are here, now, and you are safe.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). It goes deep into the parts work I am describing here, and it can help you make sense of why your body reacts the way it does.

Small practical steps for your nervous system

So what do you actually do when you feel that flinch coming? First, you stop. You do not try to override it. You do not tell yourself to calm down. You just notice. You notice the sensation in your body. The tightness, the heat, the urge to move. You name it. "I feel my shoulders tightening. I feel my breath getting shallow. I feel a wave of heat in my chest." Naming it changes the relationship. You are no longer the reaction. You are the observer of the reaction.

Second, you orient. Peter Levine talks about orientation as a key part of completing the stress response. You look around the room. You find three things you can see. A lamp, a window, the color of the wall. You find two things you can hear. The hum of the refrigerator, the sound of your own breath. You find one thing you can feel. The weight of your body in the chair, the texture of your clothing. This brings your nervous system back to the present. It tells your body "we are here, not there. We are safe."

Third, you resource. You find something that soothes your nervous system. Maybe it is a deep breath, slow and long. Maybe it is placing a hand on your chest or your belly. Maybe it is getting up and moving, shaking your hands, walking around the room. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The deep pressure stimulation can help regulate your nervous system and reduce the intensity of those startle responses.

Fourth, you do not judge yourself for needing these steps. This is the hardest part. We want to be over it. We want to be the person who does not flinch. But healing is not about never flinching again. Healing is about what happens after the flinch. It is about the moment you notice, the moment you breathe, the moment you remind yourself that you are not broken. You are just a person whose nervous system learned something it should not have had to learn. And now you are teaching it something new.

If noise sensitivity is part of your experience, a discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower the volume of the world without blocking it out completely, which can help your nervous system stay regulated in overwhelming environments.

The work is slow and that is okay

I want to be honest with you. This work is not fast. It is not linear. Some days you will feel like you are making progress and then someone will raise their hand and you will flinch just as hard as you ever did. And that is okay. That is not a failure. That is your nervous system doing what it does. The question is not whether you flinch. The question is what you do next.

Do you shame yourself? Do you spiral into "what is wrong with me"? Or do you take a breath, put a hand on your chest, and say "I see you. I hear you. We are okay."

I know which one I am working toward. And I know which one is harder. But I also know that every time I choose curiosity over shame, I am rewiring my nervous system. I am building new pathways. I am telling my body that it is safe to come back to the present.

Sit with that. The next time someone raises their hand near your face, notice what happens. Not with judgment. Just with curiosity. What does your body say? What does that part of you need? And can you give it a moment of your attention, just this once, without trying to fix it or make it go away?

That is the work. It is not glamorous. It does not make a good Instagram post. But it is real. And it is yours.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link)
